I'm Oran Klaff and we're here in pitch mastery talking about pitch anything and the strong method so we did S in strong which was set the frame and now we're on to T which is tell the story and then after this will be an R reveal the intrigue O offer the prize and nail the hook point and G get the deal strong. So I think it's very important and this is maybe the most understood part of pitching or giving presentations is the story. Everyone's focused on tell a story and let's maybe drop back and think about story a little bit you know from our ancestors perspective our ancestors evolved to live in groups you know hunter gatherers and they had to make sense of increasingly complex social relationships right they started living in communities and living in a community requires keeping tabs on who the group members are and what they're doing and how they relate to you and this is why storytelling developed this is how we developed the skill of storytelling right and to this day we still act like hunter gatherer people in a way in that we spend most of our conversations telling personal stories and gossiping and even CEOs of hundred million dollar companies spend much of their time in what scientists would call gossip why because stories do a great job of conveying emotion and getting people to empathize with you and to consider your we talked about frames or point of view right gets people a story gets people to experience something the same way that you experienced it that's very helpful and this is probably why I hear people give the advice when you sell tell a story when you make your presentation tell a story when you when you pitch your deal to investors right make sure to tell a story right really most of the business stories I hear are not good I hear a lot I hear them every day pitches they're not good and in fact really most are bad they're really bad because they're put together for the wrong purpose right most things that people think are stories are just actually well formed arguments a couple little interesting tidbits in it they're not stories right so if you're going to a business meeting yes you do need a story and in fact my own method strong ST the T stands for tell a story but I'm here to tell you why and what kind of story you actually need to tell right clear up a lot of misconception and really and since we're here working on pitch mastery which is really the pro level stuff let's call a story what it is a narrative that's what scientists call it so a business people call it so what ad agencies right and venture capitalists and the guys who really do business core to the to the you know movement of money in the economy they call that narrative business narrative there are there are a couple reasons why you tell a story and it's not what you think right it's not so people understand your product and it's not so people like you and it's not to build emotional rapport right there are much more effective ways to do these things we talked about many of them in framing in a business setting a narrative has four purposes we talk about it right now number one narrative transport the reason I will tell a story in a business meeting is that somebody else can repeat that story when I'm gone that's the job of a story might be the number one job if nobody in in business deals or in large economic transactions hardly ever able to make a decision on their own they have to hear what you're talking about and go tell it to other people they live in an ecosystem they have accountants and lawyers and it's and and they might tell it to their wife or their family I mean some of the biggest deals are killed because people go home and then at the end of the day they can't explain what it is to the wife and their wife goes it doesn't make any sense to me and the business guy gets kind of shocked back into a different state of reality goes yeah you know it doesn't make sense I can't can't explain it to you why why should I be able to do this deal the business story has to have narrative transport you tell it to someone and they have to be able to go tell it to someone else I'll give you an example a deal I'm working on so so we are flying our corporate jet into San Diego Airport the pilot there's three of us on board the pilot comes on he says guys sorry we have to divert to Palomar Airport which is 45 miles to the north right so so why is it weather he says no they're busy we couldn't get tower clearance does that mean they're busy well San Diego Airport San Diego International Airport is the busiest single runway airport in the world even busier than Gatwick Airport in London they're at capacity and what they will do is they'd rather land they're playing with 170 people on it than a plane with three people on it so charter and private jets they send up north so we spend eight thousand dollars more for the hour it takes to circle around and get a landing pattern at Palomar Airport and we land and we're an hour away from where we actually want to be so we've we've spent eight thousand dollars more and we're an hour away from our meeting the day is ruined okay now you or anyone can repeat that throughout an ecosystem hey you know San Diego Airport is at capacity these guys got rerouted it cost them eight thousand bucks they ended up 45 miles away all right it's repeatable and understandable and it has narrative transport the second reason you will tell a story again not to build rapport and all that stuff to anchor your situation your business situation your proposition in reality with what I call psychological realism psychological realism everyone around us has a natural lie detector for psychological realism we can tell when something rings false I give you an easy example the lion woke up in her cave and said I'm hungry she heard her cubs going meow which means I'm hungry too and she looked out of the cave and in the distance saw a zebra she got up in her haunches and shook her head and screamed off across the plane chased the zebra chase it around for an hour couldn't catch it tired rest a little bit look around sees an antelope nose antelope are slower than zebra at this time of the day restored she chases after the antelope jumps on it the antelope puts up a fight and claws dig in she drags into the ground and kills it with a bite to the neck and blood gushes out and she drags it three quarters of a mile back to the cave little cubs go great meow and they start digging in and eating it right and they eat and they have their fall and half of its left and they live for in a month and a lion family lives for another 90 days in the harsh reality of the serengeti okay look I don't know anything about lions this is and just you know I made it up on the spot you know anything about this stuff but it feels real doesn't it because it happens with psychological realism for whatever reason you know this is a true story it happens every day probably we just know this stuff okay so we have good lie detectors you're going to anchor your deal in reality by telling a story that is that is feels real to people because it happens in human timescales in human understandable physical environments right feels real that's the second reason to make it feel real so the third reason again we're not looking for rapport or or or um you know have people know that we're good storytellers or that we're funny right or that we're interesting or just to fill the silence we're not going to do any of those things the third reason we're going to avoid putting someone else's mind into paradigmatic mode paradigmatic mode and all that it's a big word just means cold analysis right stories bring people over to the narrative side of thinking right they don't put them in cold analysis there's a you can look them up there's a great a scientist I've talked to him okay brooner brooner wrote about two modes narrative and paradigmatic the paradigmatic mode is the mode of science and it's concerned with logically categorizing the world categorizing analysis cold stuff the other mode brooner called the narrative mode right not called that as well is concerned with with building meaning and ascribe human experiences through stories right stories are human or human like intention and action and the consequences that mark the course of people's lives all right they capture people's explanations about what they want and how they'll go about achieving it so if we put people in a logical mathematical analytical paradigmatic mode right they will be grinding us they'll be grinding us and constantly testing the things we're saying for truth and falsehood and mathematical correctness right and it won't become interested in us as characters and believe that we are honest and truthful and aspiring to bring them value they'll be constantly questioning trying to trip us up right when you put people in paradigmatic mode the analytic mode right you become the beta because they will see themselves at the alpha right the alpha doing analysis and trying to figure out if what you're what you're talking about is true and real right so each mode paradigmatic and narrative has significant strengths but they are incompatible they're incompatible right paradigmatic mode the last thing I'll say about it it it offers up the power of prediction right so it is useful right when you're going to decide how much money you're going to make next year in your business or your household or what it costs to go on vacation or buy a copier for your buildings right you can use paradigmatic mode because it it sets up hypotheses and the the nature of reality and what will unfold in the future and likelihoods and statistics and and probabilities right but in contrast the narrative mode the storytelling mode organizes complex and an ambiguity complexity and ambiguity into the human world of interaction and action puts it in a meaningful structure people can understand these two modes of thought are not complementary and they differ radically okay if you put someone in paradigmatic mode you're gonna have trouble influencing your deal because they're gonna make many decisions for themselves you put somebody in narrative mode they're gonna listen to you so there's a fourth reason beyond this why we'll use a story we may not open up with it but we have it in our kind of in our toolkit it's to recapture attention when attention dwindles oh you can feel you can feel when people are fading from you or you can see it on the mouse screen they start to click away you know or they they go somewhere else you know there's a definite sense either on technology in the internet or in person around the phone when people are fading away and at that time you can introduce a a story because when you talk about humans doing something people become interested when you talk about abstract notions people's attention fade very quickly so you'll use it to recapture attention when it dwindles right so you know the the the way we can talk about this later but there's very easy ways to do that you can say hey this reminds me of the time that we were faced with a decision like you right and we made the wrong decision really screwed us up you know and that will that you could tell a little story about that and people will suddenly you know pay more attention so let's let's summarize this and move into how to do it this is the why and what it's used for next we're going to go into how right a good story and a well-formed argument are different things right good story and a well-formed argument are different things as we've been saying both can be used as a means for convincing i'm not saying you abandon right talking about your product in technical terms or talking about the financial benefit of what you offer or selling your deal on the merits of the finance right those are well-formed arguments and they have their place and we're going to talk about that but a good story you know is is different so arguments convince people that something is likely to happen a well-formed argument convinces people that something is likely to happen okay stories stories narratives convince people that what you are talking about is true and they bring you they bring people in they bring you in they bring people in well-formed arguments are about providing empirical proof a narrative establishes your truthfulness and that's the difference we've learned a little bit about the purpose of the story and we're going to get real specific here shortly the exact steps to take the steps i take that i get narratives that go raise hundreds of millions of dollars so awareness tie i have my hair like this and i have to wear a suit okay not because i'm i do anything else this is what i do is i find narratives i take them into boardrooms i present them to people who control money and try and get them to give me the money i'm not a surfer okay at riding motorcycles i don't do very often i don't really have any other skill right the only reason i don't go camping tell stories around the fireside i don't run a kindergarten help little kids understand you know how to deal with the world i'm not a bartender that listens to your problems or tells you about mine the only reason i care about this stuff you can make money with it you can make more money the next guy who is delivering information in a really crappy way that's disorganized and has a disconnect with the human condition so i care about this stuff make money with it all right i don't know anything else they can ask me anything out of the studio talk about anything else i'll say i don't know anything else but i do know how to craft narratives that make money so let's go deeper into it you know again people say tell your story tell your story tell your narrative why so people understand you better right and i will call you out on that or anyone who says stories how people understand better that is not accurate all right people are really damn smart they understand i'll give you a quick example if i say to you for companies with large buildings in california who are dissatisfied with their aging solar panels my product is a plug-and-play solar accelerator provides 35 percent more energy than old panels and unlike the cost of getting rid of old panels my product is plug-and-play you just put it on there it's inexpensive and it has no moving parts save 35 put it on your existing solar panels save 35 earn your money back within a year there's no story there right you understand that anybody would right stories narratives don't explain stuff they don't make stuff easier to understand stuff is easy to understand products are easy to understand oh you have ball bearings and they spend a little bit faster because they have a teflon coating right and they're they're a little bit more expensive but they last 10 times as long i get it please don't wrap this in a story or a narrative my god right you have a squirrel nut scratcher and it's made out of harder plastic and it lasts four more years and it's 25 cheaper my god i get it took me four seconds to describe it please don't put narratives around stuff that don't need it most stories are boring because they aren't real stories they're timelines of unusual events we give you a good one this russian oligarch prokolov owns the nets right you know he made his billions he was a partner in a in a mineral concern in russia and they owned a lot of kind of uh manufacturing and mineral assets was on 60 minutes and so uh at some point he was running around with women and being a spectacle in the french and swiss newspapers and his partners came to him and they said sorry we got to kick you out you're too high profile we don't like this pr we're getting because of the the way you're in the papers all the time with women and drinking and god knows what so we're kicking you out take your shares we're gonna buy them back from you right they paid him billions for it and then and this is a great part the downturn the financial downturn hits and they all lose a huge part of their money and prokolov is sitting there with billions because he was kicked out because he ran around with women and was drinking in switzerland i love it but it's not a damn story right it's a timeline of unusual events that are a little bit salacious and provocative you little ping a dopamine it's not a damn story that has any use so what is a story story is something with a character we care about who is in high stakes situations and he's trying to rise above his circumstance and and find that fine line between good and evil and create a better world for both himself and people around him and there are consequences to his decisions and none of those decisions are easy right that's a narrative they do this in the walking dead right we talked about this before they uh they put the character in these these decisions should i should i operate on my son with a butter knife and a naspin or should i walk four miles to the abandoned hospital that's full of zombies flesh eating zombies and try and find some penicillin and some gauze right high stakes decisions that have life and death consequences right that's embedded in the story right another thing that i think is good and we're going to talk about this throughout strong and pitch mastery but quickly story narrative narrative story puts you on a narrative arc you know where you are like a game of golf right to get to hole five you have to have gotten to hole four and hole three and hole two and so it when you're on a narrative arc you can take someone through increasingly interesting circumstances and dramatic tension and you have you have things to hit and you know where you are because as we get to later in strong right the the nail the hook point and get the deal you have to know where you are along the narrative arcs this is going to be important the way i tell a story is probably different than how you do it right my story goes like this here's the big idea if you could do this amazing thing right then you would make lots of money but it's not easy and and here's why it's not easy but we're going to do it anyway and when we do we're going to have a massive success right that's how i get into a narrative and give you an example if you could build a small airport to take hundreds of flights a day away from san diego airport which is one of the busiest airports in the world then you would have an asset that people would use day in and day out and they would pay you a lot for it and if you could build this and get a runway 10 or 15 miles close to san diego airport you could make a billion dollars from doing that all right here's another one here's a the big idea if you could do a genetic test that covers a thousand disease conditions drug interactions inherited disease potentials and fitness nutrition and exercise results for 350 dollars you could provide that for 350 you'd be saving people 150 thousand dollars and giving them a chance to live better lives than they might otherwise and you could if you could do that make a billion dollars give you one more here's a big idea if you could deploy a semiconductor that solves the indoor reception problem on cell phones right so all over the world people walk into buildings pick up their cell phone and they lose reception but many of those people have no wired lines and they have no other way to get the internet if you could build a semiconductor to solve that problem my god you would have a billion dollar opportunity and these are all deals that i have crafted narratives for and you'll hear about them in the uh in pitch mastery throughout it but these are these are big i tell big stories big narratives not small ones right now you start to get characters both business characters and human characters people care about the next thing is the way i do it is i deepen the problem deep in the problem people spend too little time on the problem deep in the problem i'll give you a fun one that i just thought of in my mind as it started spinning up and wanted to help you guys understand this so so here's a problem right everybody loves chinese food love chinese food right but the only place you can get good chinese food is at pf chang's or in a restaurant if you try and cook it at home it doesn't come out right because you can't get the walk hot enough home stoves won't get hot enough to cook chinese food give it that amazing you know flavor and and and fresh walked whateverness okay it's important whateverness all right so so this is a massive problem because people all over the country i mean want to eat chinese food a lot more than they do but the only way they can do that is to spend a lot of money by going out it's too damn hard to cook at home because you can't get the stove hot enough it's a serious problem no matter how hard you try it's a heat problem it's not a trying hard problem it's not a skill problem it's not a getting the right ingredients problem it's not a training problem just can't get the walk hot enough at home right it's massive you know how many people want to eat and cook and enjoy chinese food at home they can't can't right so so the way i do it is i deepen the problem really really explore it from many angles then the next thing and i believe this is unique to me i've never seen anybody do it properly um i invented this to solve a specific problem the way i build business narratives but what i do is i admit that other people have solutions to the problem okay it's it's just not practical that i'm the only person that's come up with it so so for the walk right you know there's other ways to really uh get this chinese food you know at home you could order out have it brought in you could cook one little piece at a time right and get enough heat on one piece it might take you a long time or you could just eat crappy cooked food and pretend that it's good i mean there's ways that you could you know solve this i just think our walk heat accelerator is a better solution and better than anything else out there but there's ways to solve this today we're not the only people in the world that have thought about this then when i do it i will then move into before i tell you my solution i alluded to it the walk heat super hotness accelerator you need to understand that this just didn't come about as a flash of brilliance right the the gods didn't say it's time for oran to get a great idea oh my god the walk heat accelerator thing it's not how it works right what happened is i came upon this idea because there were forces in the market changes the success of pf chang's has changed the appetite of america for chinese food right and the integration my dad was a sociologist right the melting pot there's going to be more no more jews or atheists or or evangelist christians it's all going to meld together it's not going to be uh you know in a hundred years from now okay the intermarriage rate except maybe the almash i don't know but the intermarriage rate is too damn high and we're all just going to integrate and so the taste for this kind of this chinese food has just become cultural it's not related to um um you know in an asian um heritage right it's an american cultural taste and the ingredients are available in every supermarket and they're promoted and these that wasn't this way two or three years ago these forces are changing the market and they're building to a crescendo and now my business is going to take advantage of this opening market window super heat walk power 3000 btu accelerator device so now that's how i do it i'm gonna get pretty passionate about it it's a little different very different remember a business narrative is not is not what has happened right it's about what's going to happen people don't want to hear about the past in a business meeting you need to reflect on it and show how things are changing in the past and how that carries forward into the present and how a market window is opening right a business narrative moves from the past to the present and into the future and it combines and this is the magic right it combines the person or the people you're trying to influence or sell to or get to buy your stuff right it combines them with your narrative out of the past into the present and into the future right and i call this a future action projection you're projecting them into the story or the narrative that you have built and again if you're furiously taking notes don't worry we're going to go through this there's eight steps and we're going to do each of those eight steps in detail and you will be able to do this yourself right as i've said my narratives and soon yours will take people from the past through the present into an exciting future right and businesses do this anyway you know about a pro forma everyone says i want to see your pro forma your forward looking projections all right your forward looking projections you have to tell people what things are going to be like in the future it has to be framed nobody wants to know here's what we did last summer right unless they can know here's what's going to happen next summer like the past has to serve a purpose a narrative a story has to serve a purpose to frame what the future is going to be like right not through analysis and paradigmatic thinking but through narrative i mean think about the opposite of giving a narrative about the future last year we did a million dollars in revenue and things are changing dramatically right and next year let me tell you this i have no idea what's going to happen if you say this people will abandon you they'll sell off your stock and they'll quit your company and we'll do business with you right so the narrative has to build on the past introduce characters that people care about characters that are doing big things that are important and that people cheer for and want to succeed and those characters are harnessing changes and they understand those changes and they can provide insight on those changes and then they can bring you into that changing world and make you part of it that's the job of a business narrative i'm going to introduce you to my company chalexa and here's the big idea in 187 words think for a moment about what happens when you get a wound you expose the inside of your body to the outside world and what happens next germs bacteria and microbes flood in and this is always bad news because once an infection happens your chances of getting sick become worse and worse by the minute your body will start to fight against the infection on its own and your doctor may be able to help but there's one thing there's one issue that determines 80 percent of your success is the infection on a base of biofilm right biofilm is unfortunately named because it's not actually good for you and it's the foundation of 80 percent of human infectious diseases if you can address the biofilm problem and fix how it facilitates and causes human infection then you would have a major opportunity to lower healthcare costs the united states and even save lives this is what our product does it solves the biofilm problem and prevents infection and cures infection that already exists so my name is john smith and in the next 20 minutes you're going to hear about my company chalexa you're going to see some remarkable products that we've built and how life is pretty damn good for us now that we have very good products and customers and we have a solution that the market loves but it wasn't always so easy and it wasn't always like this several years ago when i started i was told it couldn't be done my own brother a venture capitalist told me not to invest my money my friend who ran a large hospital told me that even if i created the product successfully he wouldn't buy it but i knew this product could be created and against all this negative feedback i consistently worked on solving the wound care problem my first attempts frankly were misguided but from each iteration each new attempt to build the product i learned something new we didn't have enough money along the way for a full lab so we rented a lab and we used a lot of computer modeling and last year i had my first breakthrough it was one of the first times i realized that being stubborn can pay off risking your own money can pay off following the science and trusting your engineers can truly create a breakthrough and that's why we're here with this product today i'm gonna tell you a little bit about the problem that we solve when you get a cut or an abrasion a battle rages at the microbial level between your body's defenses and the bacteria in the wound this is a very big problem treating infected wounds is expensive and it's one of the biggest problems we have in this country 1.7 million people get hospital acquired infections that's 45 billion problem in the u.s and if you're not sure that's a real problem yet think about it this way there's 150 000 amputations annually in this country and there's 10 million chronic wounds and the problem is growing by 10 percent a year right and most of all think about it like this hospital acquired infections the problem that we work on with our product is the fourth leading cause of death in the united states so of course this problem can be solved at some level today the current solutions include removing infection which can be somewhat barbaric and you can use your imagination to think about it you can kill it infection which is a very costly and time consuming or you can cover it treat it over a very long period with dressings so these are solutions and they're used every day but they're expensive time consuming and painful our solution is to eradicate biofilm and stop infection all right think about it this way the chalexa cream it's called biolexa is a topical antibiotic ointment that prevents biofilm formation it combines two fda products that kill biofilm and kill bacteria we offer the only solution like this in the world costs us about seven dollars and fifty cents to manufacture treatment it's applicable in over 35 life-threatening bacteria situations and for the health care providers like hospitals that use it it saves 95 percent over the way they treat wounds and wound infections today now i'm very connected to this problem and some of you know i served in the marine corps and as a frontline medic in iraq and in fjallistan i've seen preventable wounds turn into serious illness and then become amputation and even death my time in the field has has connected me deeply to this part of health care and i'm driving hard to find better methods to prevent wound infection and to save lives i can't bring back the friends and family that i've lost but i can make a difference for the next generation i'm very deeply connected to this problem and it's important to me as much as we talk about this as a problem it's important to understand how this product works very very simple a nurse in a field hospital or any health care facility will admit a patient the doctor will identify an early signal of infection they do this all the time they will clean the wound with whatever treatment is available they will apply chalexa and a standard field dressing and then they will monitor that wound for the next couple weeks that's it it works that simply it's important to understand the value proposition so for large health care institutions and wound care clinics who treat millions of infected and chronic wounds chalexa is a wound care company that provides a solution that prevents bacteria from forming on biofilm and it eradicates infection our product prevents biofilm the substance responsible for 80 percent of human infectious diseases and it lets health care providers lower their treatment costs of wounds up to 95 percent compared to today's solutions so we talk about this business as 10 million people having this problem it's a 45 billion dollar business there's 150 000 amputations annually and that can be somewhat abstract i want to make sure that we all understand that this is human beings just like you it can affect substantially the quality of their lives if you are just coming out of a surgery the chalexa treatment our product can save you $50 000 or more in uncovered insurance if you get a secondary infection so it's not just the government or the military and some abstract numbers this can affect your life and your own economics the quality of your life as we go forward into the future as a company and take advantage of this market opportunity you and i will you know we may be and probably will be working together closely as the investor in our company you're going to get access to research that no one else can get you're going to see major opportunities as we work with the government the military and hospitals and we project you're going to get a five times return on your money in the next 24 months based on our achievements and the recognition we have from scientific peer groups and investors and customers we know that we're going to have full funding by the end of the month we'll have raised 10 million dollars from investors just like yourself and you as well we have the luxury of choosing good partners because of the importance of our product and how well we're doing and it isn't always like this you don't always get the luxury of choosing people but we do have that as we start to consider you and we might even say evaluate you i'm interested to hear about your knowledge in our area your experience as an investor overall really and how you could contribute to the things we're working on so why don't you spend a few minutes telling me how you can contribute to our company now that we have gotten super excited about the reasons to tell stories and and some of the process let's go through as i suggested earlier the eight steps okay step one this should be no surprise build a damn character that people care about right character that people care about and you can only do that with the big idea right because when you're telling a business narrative there are really two characters there's you and the business you and the business or the product the business and the product is important but it's not a human being so you got to build yourself as the character people can emote with and at the right time i mean think about this the fact that you went to stanford you sold the technology you were best friends with arnold schwarzzenegger and you know the business manager for barack obama it's not compelling right it's interesting it's not compelling to the business right so what is compelling we said it before a compelling character is undertaking something big with real consequences and stakes all right and that character is marshaling forces of change to render something with extremely high value and will have an important result in the lives of real people and that's understandable but hold on a second right you cannot walk in a room and say i'm a compelling character undertaking this big thing with real consequences and stakes and marshaling forces of change and i'm making a difference in the lives of real people you can't just walk in and say that you have to show it and you have to demonstrate that and the way you set the stage the way you set the stage to do this is to talk about your big idea the big idea we're in pitch mastery this is module two tell the story whenever you're going to tell a story in a business setting you have to be very very meticulous and particular that you're delivering business information and not kind of chaotic background so let's give some specifics on how to really tell a business narrative that's meaningful in a pitch as we said the first step is to build a character that people care about for myself for example my name is Orancliffe and early in my career starting 12 or 13 years ago i had to pitch many many times and my pitches were not well accepted and i received quite a lot of rejection and i lost deals and in many cases people didn't like me and it did not seem that i was cut out for this kind of work at all but i decided to persist because i was very passionate about the work of deal making and pitching deals and during the day even though i would fail and not do a good job and i experienced rejection and i my partners were uncertain if they wanted to keep me in the firm at all i would go home at night and study the principles and trying to find people who could teach me and learn about storytelling and learn about narratives and learn about economics and every day i chiseled a little bit more until after almost two years of day to day rejection i gave one pitch that was pretty good and everybody said that was a great job and we really are interested in investing in this deal and so the one of the things i pride myself on is the ability to overcome rejection and rise above adversity okay so this is a character that i believe people can care about and you can describe yourself in this way right and it kind of starts most character narratives will start like this when i got here to this line of work it was not easy right i started in the mail room i was a coal miner i was an analyst stuck in the basement i was a waitress okay when i first got here it was not easy but i was consistent in my efforts and i sacrificed i gave up things that other people may not have been willing to give up and i went into the unknown i went into the unknown and i forged ahead and i tried but i was not certain what the outcome would be and the people around me were uncertain either right and i continued to work hard in the face of this uncertainty and i held against the tide when it just seemed like this was not made for me when other people were not giving me the breaks right when it would be easy to give up i did not and then eventually as i remain consistent and i had self-sacrifice and i went into the unknown and i worked hard in the face of uncertainty and i held against the tide eventually things turned the corner and and when things got good when i moved from hard street to easy street right i did not become a lottery winner i did not um wallow in my good fortune and i didn't impress upon others how i had become successful i maintained my values and i continued to work hard and appreciate the value of money and care about the relationships around me and value family and so these are the things that remained important to me even after i became successful it's important to do this in a self-deprecating way in which you're not bragging but you're revealing to people the path you took to this place in time it's very important for them to understand the the travails that you had along the way so they can understand you as a character right in some ways you know your audience to them the the character that they're experiencing you can only be as interesting as the forces that are you've come up against by that i mean a character reveals himself by rising to the level necessary to overcome conflict i'll say that again a character reveals himself by rising to the level necessary to overcome conflict consider the character of rocky who we all know right most people forgot in the first movie he doesn't even win the big fight at the end of the movie the path of the rocky character and the story is is so meaningful that it doesn't really matter who wins the fight at the end it's the journey right rocky makes it's the journey he makes it's important in the first movie you know and in the other ones as well you know um if you think about it if rocky was presented as a guy who rose to the highest levels of heavyweight boxing right by overcoming easy to fight um you know partners guys who would just fall over if you tap them on the chin right what would we have learned about the character right what would we've learned about the depth of his character his dedication his perseverance nothing right if the people and the problems uh defeated are easily overcome the story that you're in is not emotionally involving enough for anybody to care right so the question for you is how do you show great tenacity and strength of character right and the strength of your will in some way so people can become involved with you um as part of this business narrative so we're gonna uh cover this in in several ways uh with the introduction in this section of each pitch i'm gonna do a little character introduction so you can see these values of consistency sacrifice going into the unknown working hard in the face of uncertainty holding against a difficult tide and then when things got good maintaining high values so let's move on to the next part of telling a business narrative step two talk about the problem really deep in the problem deep in the problem as i said in the market for walks no home stove from rancho santa fe to brooklyn can get a damn walk hot enough to co great chinese food and millions of people want to enjoy delicious chinese food that is well prepared myself included but they cannot because of this technology failure who invented this stuff jack welsha g if you see this come see me why the hell you build a stove doesn't get hot enough to cook food right people don't want to eat out every time because it's expensive this is a real problem right and this prevails through the entire culture of the united states deep problem think about it this way if you want to land at san diego airport in a private jet good luck prepare your checkbook and set your watch back an hour because you're about to get posed it may not let you land because they want to land a jet with 170 people in it and then send you an hour north to palomar okay it's a real problem if you're in business you got to get to your meeting or think about it this way it's january 5th you're in kansas enjoying a movie on your cell phone of your brother doing a backward flip a three rail ollie to the off camber down slide in 40 inch powder at wisler village are you enjoying this then you're freezing your ass off because you're standing outside nearly every building in north america and in the world has the indoor connection problem modern cell phone semiconductors don't do well in houses apartments and office buildings all right huge problem deep problem let's take a few minutes to talk about deepening the problem most business people know that you have to discuss your ideas in terms of problem solution and certainly any uh mba or venture capitalist or private equity guy or sales person knows that that's how you sell is problem solution what i find is that most people go too fast from introducing the problem to introducing their solution what i want to recommend is we think about the way that it's typically done is old and ineffective so literally we'll see this people will introduce a problem all right and very briefly and say so for example the problem is these roofing tiles get old in the sun too quickly these car tires wear out faster than you would want them to it's not um affordable to use batteries that only last 15 minutes in a camera and then our solution is better roofing tiles batteries that last longer car tires that last 100 000 miles so they jump very quickly from the problem to the solution and they spend lots of time in the solution and then features and benefits but i want to recommend an alternative approach right in the new approach in the one that i'm recommending right we uh so in the case of one of our deals which deals with um wound treatments the point of entry into the problem is to say hey infection is really bad so if you're a human being and you expose a inside of your body to the outside world through a wound right infection can set in that's really bad but infection is more than most people think it uh once the outside world is introduced and there is an infection the chances that infection can become um a serious health care problem that may be irreversible is a pretty good chance right and it's not just a good chance of being a problem for you it's a massive problem the united states and it's rampant through hospitals and through clinics and through all parts of the health care system right and not only is the problem um insidious if it happens to you and it's very hard to reverse and it's difficult for doctors to treat and it's a massive problem across the whole united states health care system at every level it's very hard to solve when the outside world gets into a wound right so there's a lot in our system there's a lot of exploration and deepening of the problem we give a point of entry we uh go deeper into it we give lots of specifics we give a sense that it's a very large scale problem and we spend lots of times exploring the problem and making it deeply felt and then you would think we would skip right to our great solution how we solve it but in fact we take an interim step and rather than saying we have the best solution in the world to solve this problem and we'll show you this specific deal so you can see this done we take an interim step to say hey there are solutions to this right so in some cases there are top for for wound treatment there's topical treatments there's certain kinds of dressings that work and unfortunately in some cases amputation is required but this problem can be solved today what i'd like to show you is that we have a better solution right so again we give people a point of entry into the problem we deepen it make it bigger we show that it's large we really really show that it's a difficult problem to solve and it's a large problem and it's a bad problem then we admit other people have problems only then are we ready to introduce our solution once we have deepened the problem and explored it fully hey are you at risk for any disease cancer asthma seizures due to adverse reaction to common drugs right well then be prepared to spend a hundred and fifty thousand dollars with twenty different companies that can help you to find out which wrecking ball the grim reaper has launched in your general direction bombs away right deep problems deepen the problem right step three introduce the character introduce the character right the human character yourself so once you deepen the problem then you can introduce and say this is something i'm involved with because of this reason here's my relationship to the problem and i'll give you an example that's deeply personal you know i got involved with this genetics company i suffered a personal family loss that was maybe preventable so i wanted to rise myself up and try and solve the problem everyone has of losing important family members when maybe maybe it could be prevented oh i know we'll have to raise tens maybe a millions of dollars maybe a hundred million dollars right and that the science is still at a beginning stage and i know we face major obstacles and in this company and getting customers and and having the revenues and getting the science all to work but i am making this change happen i'm part of it and i'm inviting other visionaries and partners in this grand adventure to join me because it's important that's my relationship with this problem in many pitches it's pretty obvious what your connection to the project is so for example if you're raising money for the zoo to add a new kind of birdcage or a lion's exhibit or additional kinds of zebra you know it's pretty obvious that you're not doing that for economic reasons and you do it because you're passionate about it but if you sell copiers right or you're selling a kind of security or raising money for real estate it's not always clear what your connection to the project is i think it's important to make that clear beyond i work here and it's my job to sell this copier how you are connected right so how are you connected to the problem in some cases so for example if you worked on just if you sold copiers right you may see i'm deeply connected to these these copiers you know they solve very very complex production problems and my life has been about discovering and being involved with elegant engineering solutions and when you take the cover off one of these copiers they're one of the most incredibly built elegant engineering machines on the planet you might not think so because you're just making copies of paperwork and things you need in the office but once you get in the guts of these things these are really amazing machines and i'm very excited about them these are engineering marvels right you may say as i've said before you know if you're in a health care deal you know my father passed away and i'm committed to fixing this part of the health care system that's how i'm that's how i'm connected to this project for me personally and i've said this before for many many years i made serious presentation mistakes and i'm passionate about showing others how to avoid these same mistakes so it is important to show people even if you're in a commodity how you are passionate and connected to the problem that you're solving with your product or your service or your idea right character and care about because i'm i'm going to do something great welled myself to the problem you'll be doing the same number four step four you got to stay away from the abstract if you get into it you got to bring yourself back buyers live in the real world with real stakes and you need to anchor your pitch in your business narrative in reality so it has a core so there's a core that is tied to the earth right to in a human way right tied to the earth because people understand what happens in the earth in the physical world big animal pictures human time scales physical reality actual physical things working together right there's two great ways to anchor your ideas or your product or your notions or your pitch or what you're selling two great ways to anchor them one is provide and admit there are other solutions to the problem you know what George Foreman actually has a pretty good walk it's made in china gets pretty hot and if they had no other option the superconducting mega 2000 whatever it was i invented if a meteor landed and blew that thing up i would actually buy the george foreman you know it does a decent job it's not great it's not great no one is going to trust and believe you if you say i'm a lone ranger i have single handedly discovered this problem and i'm the only one with an answer to it right so so you can't act blind to other solutions you have to admit i was uh in my early years i was an analyst and i went to caltech and jpl jet propulsion lab and laurence livermore laboratories and ucla the deepest darkest parts of their basements to look at technology and i would meet these inventors and these scientists who would be working at the one micron level never before achieved of a microchip or looking in a space with a deep space camera the low light camera so amazing you can't imagine that anybody else in the world would ever have thought about it a thousand monkeys at a thousand typewriters for a thousand years would not again think up this idea to have a low light camera that can see so far in the space you're actually seeing the back of your head by looking forward all right that's what i thought and all of a sudden i find out there's a friggin conference on this thing twice a year in chekosovakia nobody comes up with something so discreet and original that there's not another version of it you have to admit about those other versions all right another way to anchor it right is once you find yourself getting abstract tell a more traditional story the thing i've steered you away from about the problem tell it in human terms time skills and actions i'll give you a quick example nearly every business today especially if you're discussing the economics of it or the finance of it has some abstract nature and you will be required to talk about economics or engineering specifications or some way that the product works outside of the real world especially within the internet you know nothing there is physical so i think it's very important to accept this point after you have described abstract elements come back and anchor what you're talking about in reality and the way to do that is to discuss your topic in what i call human time scales things unfolding um in a in a natural believable way that we're all familiar with give you a couple quick examples so you might not want to say uh our wound care solution can heal a soldier without the risks common to battlefield injuries so that may be true and that may be accessible to some people who think analytically but i think you'd be more successful to say when the soldier smith sustained a gunshot to the arm his unit applied a wound dressing within three minutes right that's the difference between saying something abstract and anchoring something in the physical world in a time scale and in a place and in a situation i'll give you another example so so uh doctors need a faster method to contain and kill germ growth within a wound and so this is true and this is important and it's relevant to the deal and there's nothing wrong with it but you can certainly strengthen it by saying a doctor can identify a wound and then open the package break the seal unfold the dressing and apply it with no particular special training all problems should be at some point anchored in reality where there is not abstract terms or concepts but it is described in the physical world in physical terms and in human time scales this is why the business narrative is different from the personal story right because the hero is a product or service but but people don't care about products or services right they care about you so and they care about humans so so remember that as you build this in step four now move on to step five right because it's a business narrative we can't ignore the value proposition and we'll just do that using the idea introduction pattern that's in the book right very easy for target customers who are dissatisfied with the current offerings in the market my idea and my product is a new kind of category and it provides the following solutions to the following problems and has the following features and unlike all these crappy competing products my product has feature one and feature two and feature three and feature four we talked about this before i'll give you the same examples you can hear it again for companies with large buildings in california and in arizona who are dissatisfied with their aging solar panels my product is a plug and play plug and play solar accelerator that provides 35 more energy from old existing panels and unlike the cost of replacing old panels my product is inexpensive and guess what it has no damn moving parts great okay 35 savings has no moving parts and use your existing infrastructure i'll give you another one the value proposition i want to review one more time how to deliver the value proposition clearly this is a mistake that many companies and people selling products make is not to have and one particular point a clear identification of exactly what the value proposition is there's many different ways to do this the jeffrey moore or the idea introduction pattern is one very good way to do it i'll show you two here in this example but again the idea introduction pattern that we commonly use is for customers of a certain kind who are dissatisfied with the current offerings that are in the market our idea is new and it's a new category and it provides really these features and benefits and unlike the other products in the market our idea has these important and different features and is that other products don't have so now i'm going to give you how i've implemented that specifically in some real deals in the chalex pitch deck which you should be getting familiar with at this point and is included as part of this training the value proposition that i worked out reads like this for large healthcare institutions and wound care clinics and long-term healthcare facilities who treat millions of infected chronic wounds and who struggle with the 17 billion in annual costs related to lower extremity amputations due to chronic wounds and who have further costs from buying treatments that are at best ineffective and at worst make infections worse chalexa is a wound care company that provides a solution of two fda approved products that prevent bacteria from forming a defensive matrix and then eradicate infection our product prevents the substance responsible for 80 percent of human infectious diseases and allows healthcare providers to lower overall treatment costs up to 90 percent for certain kinds of hospital acquired infections next i'll move you into a more difficult application of a value proposition it's not always product related so in some cases where you might have a real estate project it doesn't fit the idea introduction pattern exactly and i'll show you another treatment of delivering a very very clear value proposition so in our north dakota deal which you should be coming familiar with as well and it's provided in the training and we've gone over previously my approach to this is as follows so across the portfolio that we're selling rents are currently two to three hundred dollars below market right the market rents in western north dakota will continue to increase for the foreseeable future right we have a a track record of successful rent lift you know rent lift of two to five hundred dollars and you know as every unit turns over so someone leaves the unit and a new person rents it then we increase the rents significantly right the housing demand right accounts for the growth we're having in the portfolio we maintain relationships and contracts with companies that need the corporate units that we provide specifically our housing services achieve a 50 margin and two times market rates and that's approximately one thousand two hundred fifty dollars for an efficiency apartment so again we achieve a 50 gross margin on profit we get two times or better than the existing market rates and that is exactly one thousand two hundred fifty dollars for an efficiency apartment so in this example while you might now be familiar with real estate for people who are and who are looking to buy this kind of thing it's a very very specific description of the value proposition in the deal so at some point in your deal and this is as you're in this point in the deal or selling your product or your service or introducing your idea you've got to be crystal clear about the value proposition it's a value proposition remember these are about numbers and abstract things we can all get very lost in the numbers and so you risk triggering what is the paradigmatic paradigmatic mode right and that is obviously the analytical mode when faced with too much financial data so then we have to go into step six step six bring it back to big animal pictures human scale human time frames human stories right if the numbers are exciting and compelling and the economic case is really interesting and you can see how the numbers tie together right you still have to communicate how this affects real people okay you do that very easily but you say you say this stuff affects real lives and we see it every day in our business right even even in the paper in the copier business right we affect real lives we don't just sell paper and copier and make money and go home and buy BMWs and travel to Hawaii once a year we affect lives we've said before right think about this if you're going to give a presentation to sell your company to the board that's making the decision what price to pay or you're going into walmart the next day you're proposing they buy a million of your widgets right and the night before your cap your your copier jams right your paper jams and the copier jams and it's a mess and then and then you got a real problem you have to go to FedEx kinkos and they print it wrong and you're going to meeting tired and you forgot your narrative right copiers make a big difference don't count them out affect human lives so once you give the economics and you make the financial picture or the buying value proposition real you do have to bring it back big animal pictures big animal pictures in the pitches and the presentations that i do after i've delivered a value proposition you know there's going to be a little bit of discussion and some additional conversation about your deal and the points you want to make some additional points then you have got to bring it back to human terms human time scales again many of the deals we all have are abstract in nature and so i want to show you one solution that we've developed to keep a deal even a financial deal which has no physical pieces to it giving it some human dimension so again often the discussion of conceptual and abstract topics such as market size earnings before tax and depreciation EBITDA and other mathematical jargon will trigger this paradigmatic mode in the mind right the paradigmatic mode leads to cold analysis and you never want to put the other person for a long period of time in cold analysis of your deal keep your explanations grounded in the physical world so in a genetic diagnostics company one way to do this is we show how a sample we just talk through or show how a sample moves through the lab in very plain human terms the patient spits in the plastic cup and we get a saliva sample that sample is FedExed to the processing lab robotic arms were and move and spin and they rise up in the air and they grab the sample they open the lid and they extract the DNA the DNA is then put in a machine that is much like a scanner that you would have on your desktop and the DNA is extracted and analyzed from the robotic arm to the scanner then a diagnostic report is generated in the same way that you might generate a budget from an excel file or from quicken the report is then email to the patient and the patient opens up his email in the morning and he sees an email from the company he opens it up a pdf is seen and he hits print and outside of the printer comes the DNA analysis so this is the process by which the patient gets the value proposition in other complex models or theories we have to go into even more graphic ways to explain abstract concepts so for example in the management of a venture group we came up with this solution right in a game if you hit a deal right you get a 12 times return so if you can hit one of those black boxes you get a 12 times return on your money right but hitting those is somewhat random and it's very hard if you don't know where the black spots are to get it right okay almost no one randomly choosing has a better than one in 10 so it's about a 10 percent hit rate right and many people who try and throw darts at the market to try and hit these black boxes right they go zero for 10 so it's very risky to invest in startups because startups mimic this process of throwing darts at a dartboard very similar sometimes you can throw 10 darts and hit a black spot zero times okay so how do you get winners if you're investing in a market how do you get to the winners well there's this theory called the black swan theory right that black swans are very rare events but they're very profitable so instead of looking at as a a board that you throw darts at let's look at it as a spinning wheel of chance right and you only make money when you spin on black or gray right your gut tells you that the wheel looks like a bad bet but the math says it has a high return so you you spin the wheel right and will you hit a black swan in five spins maybe not will you hit it in 10 spins maybe in reality you need 125 spins to hit a swan you know to earn a consistent high return so here you can see how we described the black swan investment model in a very physical way and until we did it with this construct it was difficult for investors to understand but with this uh caricature and show how this investment schema might work in the physical world the aha factor went up many many times and investors did in fact come to the deal when previously they couldn't understand it from just a description step seven what's going to happen that is good to the person you are pitching or selling or influencing i call this a future action projection right we have talked about the past in our narrative we have taken them into the current talked about what's happening the three moving forces i think we covered it in framing but we might as well do it here right when you describe your idea your project your product whatever it is you first give it context by framing it against market forces and trending patterns right so so if you want to talk to somebody about what's going to happen to them in the future you have to explain how the present is changing and you can you can contextualize in terms of economic forces what has changed financially in the market right for the product you sell it's credit more available or financial optimism higher where there's been increases and decreases in buying rates or interest rates or inflation or the value of the dollar or whatever these are all examples of economic forces and then you can talk about the trending of social forces right as i did in the walk example you highlight what emerging social changes there are in people's behavior and what patterns exist to make your idea fit in the in the movement of people's desires right an obvious example is the market for cars so concern over the environment is a social force and that's driving people to get these pre-issues and these these leafs and whatever these things are right that that get lots of mileage and protect the environment and the another force another driving forces technology forces like technological forces can flatten an entire existing business model and and flatten entire industries right as demand shifts from one product to another look at amazon you know just ask borders if technology can have any impact on economics they're gone amazon and electronics and the internet changes rapid and constant right you can talk about these changes so that's how the past moves into the present but then you're going to add the following here's where i am within those changes here's where i am within those changes and how i play with the social and economic and technological changes and here's what's going to happen next and this is the amazing part and if there's one thing where you really got to pay attention you then at this step the future action projection you integrate them into the story right they become part of the story you talked about what happened in the past you came into being all the moving forces and now where it's going where it's going the market window open you are the buyer of walks my walks you're the buyer of my genetic tests right and you're going to have this amazing financial future from getting involved with me because i'm in control of these forces and control of these forces and i'm i'm in an amazing narrative that you want to be part of right so you're going to paint the picture of of the future they're going to have with you many times we talk about a product in the present and what benefits the buyer or the audience is going to get from it but i think a better way to do this is by constructing a future action projection what this does is it explains the idea in the past it explains what trends are affecting it in the present and what the vision of the future will be like and then we can tell the audience or the buyer how they're going to participate in the benefits of the future based on seeing how things have changed from the past through the present and into the future the easiest way to do this is to talk about change in terms of three moving forces and almost always you'll be able to describe any idea or product or service that you have with these three forces economic forces right they're always changing what's changing the financial markets our customers have more money it's credit more available is the price of oil going up are there increases in interest rates all these things affect markets and people's ability to buy and their desire to buy products and services social forces so of course there's the social impetus to buy cars that are more efficient with gas right or people want to be more socially respectful of the environment and they want to recycle okay people are more likely to save money today after the downturn so what social forces are driving markets and of course technological forces right there are technological changes obviously in the internet in um microprocessors becoming smaller and smaller you'll be able to do more interesting things in health care the rise of genetic diagnostics and molecular diagnostics is changing all the health care and in fact i'll show you the application of a future action projection in that exact field so very quickly in genetic diagnostics there are three drivers in the market first of all there's the increase of adverse health conditions in the general population things like diabetes cancer and obesity are on the rise second of all the change in the cost of doing diagnostic the technology is getting so much better the first sequencing of a human genome was a million dollars the second was a hundred thousand dollars and the third was and how it is today approximately a thousand dollars so technology has changed it from a million to a hundred thousand to a thousand dollars of big dropping costs and lastly a huge driver is there's been over 20 billion dollars investment in publicly available information in genetic research these are the things that are driving the market now if you invest in this deal with us what you're going to find in the future you're going to be part of a world in which patients take drugs that are effective for them and they can avoid adverse reactions you know there's some drugs that cause instant deafness in children a certain kind of antibiotic and in the future all children will be able to avoid taking this drug because they will have had a genetic test very early in their life so here you can see the future action projection and work and there's a last step to doing this you're going to add step eight add dramatic tension so this is not like a movie right you cannot say to the investor what happened in the past last quarter our product orders were through the roof but then one of our engineers quit you know my god our company went into enormous turmoil we didn't know what to do we all got together around the fireside and we came up with this great idea and we deployed the great idea and guess what it worked it worked and we recovered and now we're selling more than ever that's a terrible story right because it gives you low status yes you did something great it's a terrible narrative it gives you low status you know what tell people in the 11th hour you know like you might in a movie you succeeded and pulled it out and did good things that doesn't serve your business narrative because people are not going to pay you for a description of this roller coaster ride you're going to pay you for certainty for your reliability why do people go to mcdonald's all over the world because they know exactly what they're going to get certainty when you get to the end of your pitch you're going to introduce something called dramatic tension and if you don't it can all fizzle let me tell you what I mean you may have done everything right up to this point so you built a character that people care about you've deepened the problem so they really feel the problem that you're working on you've made your solution well understood you've stated your connection to the problem why you're involved you've anchored it in reality you've delivered a value proposition so people clearly understand what you have and how the benefit from it you've brought it down to human scale so it feels personal and that this is not an abstract solution for people out there somewhere but real human beings use this and you've built a future action projection which shows people how they'll be working with you in the future and how they'll get benefits from doing that all very basic stuff but if you don't add dramatic tension it can all go to waste and here's what I mean most people when they finish their presentation or their pitch will say something like so thanks for listening or they'll say so that's what we have are there any questions and so the natural response from buyers and the audience is that was a great presentation I just saw it was given by a very smart person now I'm gonna have to show him how smart I am and the questions will start flowing and these questions will naturally try to diminish your product or your idea or services the way they can make themselves look smart too is by pointing out the weaknesses in your product or your service they'll suggest flaws in your plans so you are not gonna ask at the end of your presentation are there any questions you're not going to defend your ideas you're not going to explain yourself in any more detail and you're not going to seek validation or approval you're going to say something like hey I'm happy I could give you some of my time today now I'm very interested to explore some things you know of course is the timing right for us to work together I'm very interested is the chemistry right so I want to hear from you some things in the time we have left I'd like to know are you a good customer I'm very busy as you can see we're the best in our field we have the best product and service and we're in demand and so it means a lot to me when I take on a customer so I want to hear from you what kind of customer are you would you be a good jv partner are you good to work with have other people made money working with you you know we're not a charity yes we want to sell you this stuff and we want to have you as a customer but do other people make money when they work with you and I think this is very important to me too how do you add value to a business relationship even if you are the buyer so you're going to ask your audience or your target or your customer or your potential jv partner or your investor these questions how can they add value to you as part of the work you're going to do together as part of that transaction and that's what I mean by adding dramatic tension make them justify that they are good to work with because clearly you've given a great pitch you've established value you know how to be a good business person and a partner now you want to hear from them what kind of business people they are and what kind of partner they are by adding this dramatic tension you won't invite people to ask questions this is a very important last step in the process so dramatic tension doesn't come from from telling this wild story about how you had pains and failures and successes and and I had a roller coaster ride it comes from something totally different dramatic tension comes from pushing back on the other person and saying are you good enough to be part of my story are you good enough to be part of the future that we're building are you good enough to be part of this narrative okay because of these changes that I've described to you social economic technological there's a window of opportunity that is opening and just like in the matrix you now face a decision point you can take the red pill or the blue pill if you take the blue pill then you are doing the right thing you'll be joining us you'll be making good things happen for the environment for the business you'll be having fun you'll be you'll be joining our ranks and you'll be in on the grand adventure and you and I will seek to overthrow these sucky problems that we've talked about today and we will do something important and have fun and life will be good and easy we'll be friends and you'll make money the clouds will part and the weather will warm up all right your ailments will be cured I'm not sure we have to go that far right but but take the blue pill if you take the red pill then you go on your own damn adventure and you watch the rest of this story unfold with me at the center of it making money and having fun you'll watch it from the sidelines all right so how do you say this in business terms I think you do it like this we have a limited number of customers and I have a huge value that I can deliver okay limited number of customers we can't take everybody on can't sell that everybody I deliver a huge value to my customers and I can only work with customers who do things the right way so I'd like to hear from you right now how do you see us working together do you do things the right way all right if you do everything else right one two three four five six seven when you get to step eight you can do this right you cannot walk in the room without doing steps one through seven and say hey you bastard you want to join me right I wonder do you do things the right way I can only take on so many customers I'm not sure if you're going to be the right one can't do that is step one that's step eight all right so so this when you do this it forces your audience to understand that they have something to lose that you might go into this future that you've painted without them and it makes them pay attention they become invested because now after giving them all this value this insight this understanding this wonderful narrative just being in your presence as you tell this this magnificent story you are going to subtly remove it and withdraw it and that instinctively is going to make people reach for it remember we chase that which moves away from us we want that which we cannot have and we only value those things that we work for so those are the eight steps I'll give you an example of this being done exactly in the way I do it in another video and there'll also be a worksheet that takes you through this specifically just a quick review the steps are build a character people actually care about come on deepen the problem don't describe the problem and then russian explaining how you solved it deepen the problem you know make it deeply felt introduce characters who have high stakes and things to win and lose yourself as a character how did you become involved with this deep problem and the product okay anchor all of this step four anchor all of this in reality can't disappear into this abstract discussion of finance and and what the product does and features step five deliver the value proposition in clear and certain terms do not screw this up I will come get you it's not hard to do all right don't mess that up step five step six after you've talked about the features and the benefits bring it down to human scale give it a truthfulness seven step seven build this future action projection the future is going to look like this and I'm going into it I might go in there without you and that's step eight wrap it all up in dramatic tension okay you can do that by by asking the customer what kind of customer he is and can he play on this field of play that you're on with the pros you'll start to move away a little bit and we're going to talk about that next in the in the R in the strong method we're going to talk about revealing the intrigue where you'll be pulling back and moving away and having them chase you but you're not going to be able to reveal the intrigue until you do this right telling the story remember and this is my final point it's not telling a story it's telling the story so we'll move on then to the next video in a few and I'll see you when we talk about our revealing the intrigue
